question_title: Will the Trump administration formally notify Congress of the pending ~$14 billion Taiwan arms package before May 31, 2026?
summary: The $14 billion Taiwan arms package has NOT been formally notified to Congress as of May 22, 2026. Trump explicitly stated he hasn't decided, said he would speak with Lai first (no call scheduled), and framed the sale as a 'negotiating chip' with China. China is actively pressuring Trump via blocking Colby's Beijing visit. The compound probability of Trump calling Lai, deciding to approve, AND the formal notification being processed and published within the remaining 8 days is very low (~6%). While 'couple of weeks' from May 15 technically reaches May 29, Trump's pattern of delays, the absence of any reported preparatory activity, and active Chinese diplomatic pressure all point strongly toward NO resolution within this narrow window.

factors.description: Trump explicitly stated he has 'not yet made a decision' and would decide in the 'next fairly short period' - no concrete timeline given (as of May 21)
Trump said he would speak with Taiwan's Lai first, but CNN reports 'It is not clear whether the Trump administration has moved forward with any plans for a call' - prerequisite not met
China actively pressuring Trump - blocking Pentagon Under Secretary Colby's Beijing visit until Taiwan arms decision is resolved (FT, May 20-21)
Trump framed Taiwan arms sales as a 'very good negotiating chip' with China - suggesting strategic delay rather than quick action
Only 8 days remaining in window (May 22-30). Even after presidential decision, formal DSCA notification requires bureaucratic processing and publication
White House omitted Taiwan from official summit readout - signaling deference to China on this issue
Strong bipartisan Congressional pressure (May 8 Senate letter, PORCUPINE Act advancing unanimously 45-0 on May 21, bipartisan support)
Trump said 'couple of weeks' on May 15, which could technically mean by May 29 - within the window, and December 2025 precedent shows administration CAN move quickly

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Probability: 6% (NO is strongly favored)**

### Key Reasoning:

1. **No decision made yet**: As of May 21, Trump explicitly stated he hasn't decided and would make a determination in "the next fairly short period" — vague and non-committal.

2. **Prerequisites not met**: Trump said he'd speak with Taiwan's President Lai first, but CNN reports no plans for such a call have materialized.

3. **Active Chinese counter-pressure**: Beijing is blocking a Pentagon official's visit as leverage, creating strong incentive for Trump to delay.

4. **"Bargaining chip" framing**: Trump explicitly called Taiwan arms sales a "very good negotiating chip" — signaling strategic delay rather than urgency.

5. **Extremely tight timeline**: Only 8 days remain. The critical path requires (a) Trump-Lai call, (b) positive decision, (c) bureaucratic processing at State/DSCA, (d) formal publication — all by May 30.

6. **No signs of imminent notification**: Despite intense media scrutiny, there are zero reports of notification paperwork being prepared.

The 6% accounts for the small but non-zero chance that Trump acts impulsively/quickly (as he did with the December 2025 package) or that behind-the-scenes preparation is further along than publicly reported.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the Trump administration formally notify Congress of the pending ~$14 billion Taiwan arms package before May 31, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43560)

[2026-0518: Trump Urged to Approve $14B Taiwan Arms Package; House Committee Advances PORCUPINE Act; House Resolution Celebrates TAHW; Taiwan’s “Non-Red” Drone Supply Chains - Formosan Association for Public Affairs](https://fapa.org/2026-0518-trump-urged-to-approve-14b-taiwan-arms-package-house-committee-advances-porcupine-act-house-resolution-celebrates-tahw-taiwans-non-red-drone-supply-chains/)

[House Foreign Affairs greenlights trio of arms sale bills, including Taiwan support - Breaking Defense](https://breakingdefense.com/2026/05/house-foreign-affairs-greenlights-trio-of-arms-sale-bills-including-taiwan-support/)
[Trump says he’s willing to talk to Taiwan’s leader, a major departure from diplomatic norms | CNN](https://www.cnn.com/2026/05/21/asia/trump-taiwan-china-lai-call-intl-hnk)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves YES if, between May 17, 2026 and 23:59 ET on May 30, 2026, a formal Foreign Military Sale (FMS) congressional notification (or Direct Commercial Sale notification) covering items from the pending ~$14 billion Taiwan arms package (e.g., missiles and/or air-defense systems for Taiwan / Taipei Economic and Cultural Representative Office in the United States (TECRO)) is published either on the U.S. State Department's arms-sales notifications page (https://www.state.gov/arms-sales-congressional-notifications/) or on the DSCA Major Arms Sales page (https://www.dsca.mil/Press-Media/Major-Arms-Sales). The notification(s) must collectively involve at least $5 billion in estimated value to TECRO/Taiwan within this window (to distinguish from small follow-on cases). Otherwise the question resolves NO. If both primary sources are unavailable, reporting from [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) confirming such a notification has been transmitted to Congress in this window may be used as a fallback.
fine_print: "The pending ~$14 billion Taiwan arms package" refers to the package widely reported in May 2026 (including missiles and air-defense systems) that Trump said he was "unsure" about after meeting Xi Jinping. The notification need not match exactly $14 billion or contain every component; any notification(s) totaling at least $5 billion to Taiwan/TECRO published in the window count. A White House or Pentagon announcement that the package has been "approved" but without a formal congressional notification by 23:59 ET on May 30, 2026 does NOT count. The earlier December 2025 ~$11 billion package and the December 17, 2025 TOW missile case do not count.

Trump said he would speak with Taiwan's Lai first, but CNN reports 'It is not clear whether the Trump administration has moved forward with any plans for a call' - a prerequisite hasn't been met
China actively pressuring Trump - blocking Pentagon Under Secretary Colby's Beijing visit until Taiwan arms decision is resolved (FT, May 20-21)
Trump framed Taiwan arms sales as a 'very good negotiating chip' with China - suggesting strategic delay rather than quick action
Only 8 days remaining in window (May 22-30). Even after presidential decision, formal DSCA notification requires bureaucratic processing and publication

Strong bipartisan Congressional pressure (May 8 Senate letter, PORCUPINE Act advancing, bipartisan support)

input.anchor: Base rate for major arms sale notification within a specific 2-week window when the president has explicitly wavered: very low. Historical FMS notifications happen on the administration's timeline, not external deadlines. Trump has a pattern of delaying stated timelines. Anchor at ~10% (accounting for the fact that he DID say 'couple of weeks').
input.assessment:
The question asks whether a formal FMS notification worth at least $5 billion for Taiwan will be PUBLISHED on State Dept/DSCA websites between May 17-30, 2026. As of May 22, no such notification has occurred.

The critical path requires: (1) Trump-Lai phone call (not yet scheduled), (2) Trump positive decision, (3) bureaucratic processing at State/DSCA, (4) formal publication - all by May 30.

Strong evidence against YES:
- Trump hasn't called Lai yet and gave no timeline
- Trump explicitly hasn't decided and framed arms sales as 'negotiating chip'
- China actively pressuring via Colby visit blockage
- White House omitted Taiwan from summit readout
- Only 8 days remain, and formal notification requires bureaucratic processing after decision

Weak evidence for YES:
- 'Couple of weeks' from May 15 = ~May 29 (barely within window)
- Strong Congressional pressure
- Trump can move quickly when motivated

The compound probability of all required steps completing within 8 days is very low. Even optimistically: P(call by May 25) ≈ 35% × P(approve | call) ≈ 45% × P(notification published in time | approval) ≈ 55% ≈ 9%. More realistically, closer to 5-6% given Trump's pattern of delays and the 'bargaining chip' framing.
input.calibration_notes: Anchor is ~10% based on Trump's vague 'couple of weeks' timeline statement. Updating downward because: (1) no concrete steps toward notification have been reported, (2) the prerequisite call hasn't happened, (3) China pressure creates incentive to delay, (4) 'bargaining chip' framing suggests indefinite delay. Final estimate of 6% reflects that while not impossible, the timeline is extremely compressed for a decision that has active diplomatic headwinds.
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Could Trump bypass the Lai call and just decide? Possible but contradicts his stated process. 2. Could the bureaucratic process be faster than expected? Yes, DSCA can move quickly, but needs a presidential green light first. 3. Could there be behind-the-scenes movement not yet reported? Unlikely given intense media scrutiny - any notification would be major news immediately.
input.tool_audit: research() - first query returned excellent comprehensive data on the package history and current status; second query failed due to tool unavailability. web_search - provided good current news coverage. fetch_url - FAPA article was highly informative; US News timed out; CNN and Breaking Defense provided good supplementary data. DSCA site search confirmed no new 2026 Taiwan notifications.
input.update_triggers: - Reports of Trump-Lai phone call occurring would significantly increase probability (to ~20-25%). - Reports of State Department preparing formal notification paperwork. - Trump statement that he's decided to proceed. - Reports of DSCA notification being transmitted.
input.process_reflection: The research tool's first query was extremely productive, returning a comprehensive timeline. The second query failed but the first compensated. Web search was effective at finding current news. The fetch_url timeout on US News was minor since the snippet already contained the key information. Overall, the tool pipeline worked well for this question - the combination of research() for background and web_search/fetch_url for breaking news coverage was effective.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

### Factor-by-factor check against trace:

**Factor 1** (Trump "not yet made a decision", logit=-1.5): VERIFIED. AP News snippet in trace: "Trump says he's not yet made a decision on whether a major sale of U.S. arms to Taiwan can move forward." The "next fairly short period" phrasing is confirmed by the News Tribune fetch: "make a determination over the next fairly short period." Grounded.

**Factor 2** (Trump-Lai call not scheduled, logit=-1.5): VERIFIED. CNN article fetch (line 484-491): "It is not clear whether the Trump administration has moved forward with any plans for a call." The prerequisite claim is well-supported.

**Factor 3** (China blocking Colby visit, logit=-1.0): VERIFIED. Multiple sources in trace confirm. Reuters via People News Today: "Beijing is holding up a proposed visit by the Pentagon's under-secretary of defence for policy, Elbridge Colby, as China pressures U.S. President Donald Trump over a $14 billion weapons package for Taiwan." FT report confirmed by Taipei Times and others.

**Factor 4** (Trump "negotiating chip" framing, logit=-1.2): VERIFIED. AP News/research entry: "Trump described Taiwan arms sales as a 'very good negotiating chip' in dealings with China."

**Factor 5** (8 days remaining + bureaucratic processing, logit=-1.0): VERIFIED. Math checks out: question resolution is May 30, today (at time of forecast) was May 22, giving 8 days. The bureaucratic processing claim is reasonable and not contradicted by any source.

**Factor 6** (White House omitted Taiwan from summit readout, logit=-0.5): VERIFIED. FAPA article fetch: "the White House omitted Taiwan entirely from its official readout." Grounded.

**Factor 7** (Bipartisan Congressional pressure, logit=+0.5): VERIFIED. Multiple sources: May 8 Senate letter (web search result from senate.gov), PORCUPINE Act advancing (Breaking Defense fetch: "approved unanimously (45-0) by the House Foreign Affairs Committee on May 21, 2026"), AP News headline about bipartisan support.

**Factor 8** (Trump "couple of weeks" = possibly by May 29, logit=+0.3): PARTIALLY VERIFIED. The trace sources have Trump saying "next fairly short period" (News Tribune) and "couple of weeks" (agent's assessment of the May 15 statement). The Bloomberg headline "Trump Says He Made No Commitment to Xi On Taiwan, Will Decide on Arms Sale Soon" is consistent. The confidence of 0.4 on this factor is appropriately low given the vagueness.

### Resolution criteria check:
The agent correctly identified what's needed: formal FMS notification published on State Dept or DSCA websites, at least $5B to TECRO/Taiwan, between May 17-30. The agent correctly distinguished this from the December 2025 package, which is explicitly excluded. The question's resolution criteria were obtained via the Metaculus API call and properly analyzed.

### Double-counting check:
Factors 1 and 2 are related (both about Trump's indecision) but address distinct aspects — Factor 1 is about the explicit lack of decision, Factor 2 is about a specific prerequisite (the Lai call) not being met. They draw from different sources (AP News vs CNN). Factors 3, 4, and 6 all relate to Chinese pressure/deference but capture meaningfully different signals (active diplomatic leverage vs. Trump's own framing vs. readout omission). Mild overlap between 3 and 6 but not severe enough to constitute meaningful double-counting.

### Wrong-direction check:
All factor signs are consistent with their descriptions. Negative logits for factors opposing notification, positive for supporting it.

### Contradictory assessment check:
The assessment concludes ~6% probability (strong NO lean), consistent with the heavily negative factor sum. The agent acknowledged the factor sum implies 0.4% but adjusted upward to 6% to account for irreducible uncertainty and Trump unpredictability. This departure is reasonable and transparent.

### Missing resolution criteria:
Resolution criteria were properly retrieved via the Metaculus API. No issue.

### Key nuance — is the $14B package separate from the Dec 2025 $11.1B?
The question background explicitly states they are separate. The FAPA article describes different contents (counter-drone assets, integrated battle command system, medium-range munitions) from the December 2025 package (HIMARS, howitzers, ALTIUS drones). CNN confirms "$14 billion arms deal was approved by Congress in January 2026." The agent correctly understood these as distinct.

### Tool audit check:
The agent claimed "research() - first query returned excellent comprehensive data; second query failed due to tool unavailability." The metrics show: research had 1 call with 0% error rate. But the *research* tool returned TWO results — the first was comprehensive, and the second entry explicitly states "I was unable to fulfill this research request due to all web search and fetch tools being unavailable." So while the research tool itself didn't error, one of its sub-queries produced an empty result. The agent's description is essentially accurate — the second query was unfulfilled, though technically the tool call itself succeeded. This is a minor discrepancy, not material. The fetch_url 25% error rate (1 out of 4 calls) matches the trace — the US News URL timed out.

### Counterargument quality:
The counterargument is substantive. It raises specific mechanisms: Trump's "couple of weeks" as genuine commitment, December 2025 precedent showing fast execution, surprise call possibility, behind-the-scenes paperwork preparation. It's not a token gesture — it constructs a coherent alternative scenario. The "what would change my mind" section adds concrete observable triggers with probability updates. Adequate.

### Confidence assessment:
0.60 confidence seems appropriate given the clear evidence against but short resolution window where surprises are possible. Not overconfident.

### Probability assessment:
The 6% estimate aligns well with the evidence. The compound probability calculation (P(call) × P(approve|call) × P(notification|approve) ≈ 35% × 45% × 55% ≈ 9%) provides a reasonable upper bound. The downward adjustment to 6% accounts for the "bargaining chip" framing and Trump's delay patterns. I don't see a meaningful divergence — my own estimate would be in the 4-8% range given: no decision made, no call scheduled, active Chinese pressure, only 8 days, and the formal bureaucratic requirement. 6% is well-calibrated.

### Anchor divergence:
Anchor at ~10%, final at 6%. The 4pp departure is modest and justified by the accumulation of negative evidence (no call, Chinese pressure, readout omission). No concern.

### Worldview consistency:
No contradictory worldview entries found. The Taiwan-related research entries either confirm the agent's narrative or were incomplete due to tool failures. No conflicting forecasts or sub-forecasts identified.

### Verdict: APPROVE
All factual claims are grounded in the trace. The probability is well-reasoned and consistent with the evidence. No hallucinated evidence, no meaningful double-counting, no wrong-direction factors, no resolution misalignment. The estimate of 6% is reasonable.
